findings.finding: Topic 9.3 refers to 'System 1 processing' (section 5, paragraph on 'Why confirmation bias is so automatic') while topics 9.4 and 9.5 consistently use 'System 1 thinking' — the canonical term introduced in topic 9.1. Same concept, different label suffix. Minor synonym drift; does not affect comprehension but is inconsistent with the module's own vocabulary convention.
findings.recommendation: Align 9.3 to use 'System 1 thinking' where the corpus currently says 'System 1 processing'.

findings.finding: The module's lab_activity_seed references the Judgment Framework and error-threshold definition — deliverables that live in modules 3 and 4 of the same week. This module (2) provides the cognitive-science grounding for those lab tasks but does not deliver the lab artifacts itself. This is correct by curriculum design, not a gap within this module, but is noted for HITL #4 rollup awareness.

Topic 9.6 intentionally re-applies terms from 9.5 (automation bias, commission error) in its Section 5.7 to show how individual cognitive biases drive structural encoding problems. This is pedagogically correct cross-referencing, not concept duplication. Jaccard similarity of core Section-5 noun sets is approximately 0.12, well below the 0.6 flag threshold.

The module lab_activity_seed ('Apply the Judgment Framework', 'Define acceptable error thresholds', 'Journal Entry #5') is served by modules 3 and 4 of week 9, not by this module's topics. Each topic in this module (9.3–9.6) contains a Hands-On/Reflection Prompt that is domain-applicable and feeds the week-level lab, but the primary lab deliverables are out of scope for module 2 by design. No hands-on artifact in this module directly demonstrates the Judgment Framework application.
findings.recommendation: No change required in module 2 content. At HITL #4 rollup, confirm that module 3 (9.7–9.9) delivers the Judgment Framework application activity that the lab_activity_seed calls for.

findings.finding: 'Proxy variable' is introduced and immediately defined inline in Section 7 of the 9.6 corpus ('proxy variables — indirect stand-in features, like zip code and credit history length, that correlate with a protected characteristic without naming it directly') but is absent from the Section 10 Key Vocabulary table. For absolute beginners, this term is substantive enough to warrant vocabulary-table inclusion.
findings.recommendation: Add 'proxy variable' to the Key Vocabulary table in the 9.6 corpus and any generated reading artifact.

findings.finding: All four topics explicitly declare that no programming or engineering background is required (9.5 and 9.6 state this verbatim in Section 2). All topics tag depth_profile as 'standard'. No topic spikes in register or depth relative to its neighbors. Consistent absolute-beginner targeting maintained across the module.

topics_checked.title: Confirmation bias — seeking information that confirms existing beliefs
Anchoring bias — over-relying on the first piece of information received
Automation bias — trusting automated systems over human judgment
How human biases get encoded into AI training data

verdict: PASS — all six drift checks passed; three soft findings (terminology synonym in 9.3, missing vocabulary-table entry in 9.6, lab deliverables by design in downstream modules) — none block topic approval
